scores.rationale: Searches on 'RL reward diversity entropy LLM idea generation' and 'distribution-matching reward research idea generation' surface general diversity-promoting RL/decoding work (e.g. diverse beam search, entropy-bonus RL) but nothing that specifically uses the arxiv-2607.01233 opportunity/paradigm taxonomy as a KL-divergence reward inside LDC's RL stage, so the core mechanism looks unclaimed even though the general idea of entropy/diversity rewards is well trodden; feasibility is moderate since it depends on LDC's code and a reliable taxonomy classifier, both of which exist but require nontrivial integration work. The 'ambitious' riskLevel is reasonably calibrated to this moderate novelty.

Prior work on predicting paper/proposal acceptance or success from linguistic/text features (e.g. peer-review score prediction, grant-success-prediction NLP models) is fairly established, and the ideation-execution-gap paper's dataset is small (43 pairs), so a gradient-boosted classifier on this exact resource is plausible but sits close to a well-known genre of 'predict outcome from text features' work, lowering novelty; feasibility is high since the dataset is public and the method is standard ML engineering, but the 43-sample size makes the AUROC>0.7 claim optimistic. Labeling this 'incremental' looks accurate given how close this is to existing proposal/paper-quality prediction literature.

No published work was found training a critic LM directly on HindSight-style future-citation/impact scores and inserting it into a multi-agent critique-revision loop in place of an LLM-judge critic; this combination looks genuinely unexplored, though outcome-based reward modeling for agents is a known pattern elsewhere (RLHF, RLAIF). Feasibility is the weak point: HindSight itself is a very recent (2026) small-scale framework (10 topics), so generating enough (idea, future-impact) pairs to fine-tune a critic is a serious data-scarcity bottleneck, making the 'high-risk' label well calibrated to both the novelty and the execution difficulty.

Searches on DPO/contrastive fine-tuning for research-question or hypothesis generation surfaced general DPO-for-creative-writing and preference-tuning-for-novelty work, but nothing that specifically builds contrastive pairs from RQ-Bench's author-anchored vs. LLM-generated RQs to train against 'narrow/source-bound' framing, so the specific data construction and training target appear novel even though DPO itself is a standard technique; feasibility is moderate since RQ-Bench data and expert narrowness labels would need to be assembled/extended beyond what RQ-Bench's original paper released. 'Ambitious' risk level tracks reasonably with this moderate-high novelty and moderate feasibility.

Scideator (facet recombination) and Deep Ideation (concept-network traversal) are each well-documented individually, and hybrid 'structured-attribute-filtered graph search' approaches exist in adjacent creativity-support literature, but no direct fusion of these two specific published systems (facet-tagged graph edges constraining explore-expand-evolve search) was found, so this sits as a genuine but modest combinatorial-novelty contribution rather than a wholly new mechanism; feasibility is moderate-to-good since both source systems have described components/code, though integrating facet extraction with graph-edge annotation is real engineering work. 'Ambitious' is slightly overstated for what is largely an engineering fusion of two existing published pipelines — this leans closer to incremental-to-ambitious.

The general pattern of 'reproduce multiple ideation systems and re-score under one standardized metric' closely resembles what GraphEval and arxiv-2601.09714 already partially attempt (the latter explicitly benchmarks five reasoning architectures under one evaluation protocol), and meta-benchmarking/re-evaluation studies are common in NLP generally, so the core framing is not far from existing practice even though no single paper has combined exactly Nova + LDC + Deep Ideation + the multi-agent dialogue system + the five arxiv-2601.09714 architectures under GraphEval-GNN specifically; feasibility is moderate-good since public repos exist for most systems but reproduction/engineering overhead across 6+ codebases is substantial. 'Incremental' risk level is well calibrated — if anything this is appropriately modest given how close prior meta-benchmarking work already is.

Bias-correction/calibration of LLM-as-judge scores against a human reference (via regression) is an active general research theme (judge calibration, debiasing LLM evaluators), but using taxonomy-cluster distance from arxiv-2607.01233 specifically as the correction feature for novelty-score calibration, tested on RINoBench/RQ-Bench, appears to be a novel combination not directly published elsewhere; feasibility is reasonably good since all three underlying resources (RINoBench, RQ-Bench, taxonomy classifier) are public, though stitching together three different papers' datasets/protocols adds real integration risk. Calling this 'high-risk' is slightly overstated relative to the feasibility of the individual components, though the novel three-way combination and the untested efficacy of the correction term justify some risk premium.
